content.full_text:
California
SUPERSFDE
Fair Political
Practices Commission
IN FART
b i- -
(SLtsSboff)
Ooctober 16,
1992
Becky Rishe
8739 Laureiwood Drive
Rohnert Park,
CA
94928
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-92—521
Dear Ms.
Rishe:
This letter is in response to your request for confirmation
and confirms the telephone advice provided to you
on October 1,
1992,
regarding your duties and responsibilities under the
conflict—of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the
“Act”) 1
Our response is limited to the provisions of the Act.
If you
are elected to office,
you may wish to consult with the Office of
the Attorney General regarding other provisions of
law such as
Government Code Section
1090.
QUESTION
Under the provisions of the Act,
may you run as a candidate
for the Rohnert Park City Council?
CONCLUSION
The provisions of the Act do not restrict your ability to run
as a candidate for a position with the Rohnert Park City Council.
However,
once you are elected to office,
the conflict-of—interest
provisions of the Act may require you to disqualify yourself from
participating in governmental decisions which will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your economic
interests.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
et g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-92-521
Page
2
FACTS
In late 1990,
you were the recipient of
a loan from the
community development commission.
You are currently a candidate
for the Rohnert Park City Council.
If you were elected to the
city council,
you would become a member of the city’s community
development commission.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in,
or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.
Councilmembers are public
officials.
(Section 82048.)
Thus, if you are elected to the city
council,
you will be a public official.
An official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally,
on the official,
or on a
member of the official’s immediate family,
2 or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within
12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
(e)
Any donor of,
or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to,
received by,
or promised to the public
2
An official’s “immediate fariaily” are his or her spouse and
dependent children.
(Section 82029.)


Our File No.
A-92--521
Page
3
official within
12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
For purposes of this section,
indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a
public official,
or by a business entity or trust
in which the official,
the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly,
or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.
Section 87103.
Absent an economic interest as defined in Section 87103,
the
Act does not prohibit an official’s participation in governmental
decisions.
You advised me that you had a community development
loan which you received in 1990.
For purposes of Section
87103(c),
a loan is
“income”
and thus potentially a disqualifying
economic interest.
However,
the Act excludes from the definition
of “income” any loan or loans from a commercial lending
institution which are made in the lender’s regular course of
business on terms available to members of the public without
regard to official status if:
(A)
Used to purchase,
refinance the purchase
of,
or for improvements to,
the principal residence
of filer; or
(B)
The balance owed does not exceed ten
thousand dollars
($10,000).
Section 82030(b) (8).
Thus, if your loan was transacted through a commercial
lending institution in the regular course of business on terms
available to the public without regard to official status,
and if
you used the proceeds to purchase,
refinance or improve your
principal residence,
the loan is not a disqualifying economic
interest for purposes of Section 87103(c).
In addition, the loan
would not be a disqualifying economic interest if the balance does
not exceed $10,000 and the loan was transacted through a
commercial lending institution in the regular course of business
on terms available to the public without regard to official
status.
Conversely,
if the conditions of the loan were such that the
loan would be considered an economic interest for purposes of
Section 87103(c),
you would have to disqualify yourself from
participating in decisions which will have
a foreseeable and
material financial effect on the lender or the security for the
loan,
if any.
You would also have to disqualify yourself from
participating in decisions which would alter the terms of the loan


Our File No.
A—92-521
Page
4
and affect your assets and liabilities by
$250 or more.
(Regulation 18702.1(a) (4).)
As I advised you during the course of our conversation, the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act do not apply to you
until such time as you are elected to the city council.
Once you
become a public official,
you may wish to contact our office
regarding potential conflicts of interest with regard to your
various economic interests.
We are enclosing some informational materials regarding the
conflict-of—interest provisions of the Act.
Should you have
further questions regarding this matter,
do not hesitate to
contact me at
(916)
322-5901.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
By:
Blanca
M.
Breeze
Counsel,
Legal Division
SH:BMB:dd
Enclosures
